1: Type / Theme. A wise son makes a glad father — the father-son relationship as the structural frame of the entire Solomonic collection. The son who receives and embodies wisdom delights the father; the fool grieves him. Christ is the Son who always does what pleases the Father (John 8:29): at his baptism the Father declares his delight (this is my beloved Son in whom I am well pleased, Matthew 3:17). Every human son who has grieved his father is repositioned in Christ — the Son who perfectly delighted the Father, whose record of filial faithfulness is imputed to the penitent who trusts him.
2: Theme. Treasures gained by wickedness do not profit, but righteousness delivers from death — righteousness as the instrument of deliverance from death. The verse anticipates the doctrine of imputed righteousness: Paul argues in Romans that the righteousness credited to Abraham through faith (Romans 4:3-5) is the same righteousness that delivers from death (Romans 5:9). Christ's righteousness is the only righteousness that actually delivers from the death the verse has in view — the wickedness-gained treasure perishes; the righteousness that delivers is received, not achieved.
3: Revelation of God. The LORD does not let the righteous go hungry, but he thwarts the craving of the wicked — the divine provision-pattern. Christ demonstrates this principle at the feeding of the five thousand (Matthew 14:13-21) and in the Sermon on the Mount's promise that those who seek the kingdom will have all these things added (Matthew 6:33). The LORD who does not let the righteous hunger is Christ who multiplies bread and who declares himself the Bread of Life (John 6:35) — the hunger Proverbs promises God will satisfy is met in him both physically and ultimately.
4: Theme. A slack hand causes poverty, but the hand of the diligent makes rich — the work-ethic observation. While a general wisdom principle, the NT applies it christologically: Paul's instruction to work with one's hands (1 Thessalonians 4:11; Ephesians 4:28) is grounded in the pattern that diligence produces the resources that enable generosity toward others. Christ the carpenter-craftsman (Mark 6:3) embodies dignified manual labor before his public ministry — the diligent hand as the normal human mode of sustaining life before the extraordinary calling comes.
5: Theme. He who gathers in summer is a prudent son, but he who sleeps in harvest is a son who brings shame — the harvest-season wisdom. The harvest as a figure for the eschatological moment of gathering recurs throughout the Gospels: Christ declares the harvest is plentiful (Matthew 9:37-38), commands his disciples to pray for workers, and uses the harvest as the figure of final judgment (Matthew 13:30, 39). The son who gathers in summer is the disciple who responds to the harvest-urgency Christ identifies; the sleeping son is the one who misses the appointed moment.
6: Theme. Blessings are on the head of the righteous, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence — the beatitude-pattern of blessing on the righteous. The Sermon on the Mount's beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12) are the NT form of this sapience observation — the righteousness of the kingdom-seeker attracts divine blessing. Christ's own head received a crown of thorns — the blessings that should rest on the head of the perfectly righteous one were momentarily replaced by the marks of the wicked's violence, absorbing what the second half of the verse identifies as the wicked's characteristic covering.
7: Revelation of God. The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot — the name-memory principle. Christ's name is the name above every name (Philippians 2:9-10), the name by which people are saved (Acts 4:12), the name that endures into eternity (Revelation 19:13). The righteous whose memory is a blessing are those who bear his name in faithfulness. The wicked whose name rots is the countertype — those whose earthly prominence dissolves against the permanence of Christ's name.
8: Theme. The wise of heart will receive commandments, but a babbling fool will come to ruin — receptivity to instruction as wisdom's mark. Christ's disciples are characterized precisely by this receptivity — they receive the commands Christ gives (John 14:21; Proverbs 15:10) and keep them as the expression of love for the Father. The fool who babbles (eiwelet sefatayim, lips of folly) contrasts with the wise heart that is oriented to receive rather than to produce undiscriminated speech.
9: Type. Whoever walks in integrity walks securely, but he who makes his ways crooked will be found out — integrity of walk as the path of security. Christ is the Way (John 14:6) — the one in whom the crooked-or-straight choice of Proverbs is resolved. The straight path is not merely a moral posture but a Person: those who walk in him walk in integrity because they walk in the one who is himself truth. The one who makes crooked ways will be found out — at the judgment, all concealment ends, which is precisely why the only security is union with the one whose walk was always straight.
10: Theme. Whoever winks the eye causes trouble, and a babbling fool will come to ruin — the deceptive wink as a social harm. Judas's betrayal-signal at the Last Supper (the gesture of identifying Jesus to his captors) is the ultimate expression of the deceptive communication this proverb warns against — the surface warmth concealing betrayal. Christ himself warned that those who say Lord, Lord but do not do his will will be revealed (Matthew 7:21-23) — the babbling fool's surface religiosity concealing an absence of genuine relationship.
11: Type. The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence — the mekor chayim (fountain of life) produced by the righteous man's speech. Christ declares: Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from his heart (John 7:38). The righteous man of Proverbs 10:11 who produces life-giving speech is a type pointing to Christ, in whom the fountain of life is not merely verbal but ontological — his Spirit poured out is the living water that springs up to eternal life (John 4:14).
12: Theme. Hatred stirs up strife, but love covers all offenses — the covers-offenses principle that Peter quotes directly (1 Peter 4:8: love covers a multitude of sins). The ultimate expression of love covering offenses is the atonement: Christ's death covers the sins of those who trust him, not by ignoring them but by bearing them. The love that covers is not sentimental tolerance but the absorptive capacity of the one who takes the offense into himself. John's Gospel makes this explicit: the greater love of laying down one's life (John 15:13) is the maximum form of what Proverbs 10:12 describes.
13: Theme. On the lips of him who has understanding, wisdom is found, but a rod is for the back of him who lacks sense — wisdom on the lips as the mark of understanding. Christ's teaching is consistently described as having authority (exousia) unlike the scribes (Matthew 7:29) — wisdom resident in his lips that comes from the divine source directly. James identifies the tongue as the test of the religious life (James 3:2-12), making the wisdom-or-rod principle of Proverbs applicable to the character of speech that marks those formed by Christ's instruction.
14: Theme. The wise lay up knowledge, but the mouth of a fool brings ruin near — storing vs. expending knowledge. The disciples' instruction is described as storing up in Matthew 13:52: the scribe trained for the kingdom brings out of his treasure what is new and what is old. Christ is himself the storehouse of all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3) — the wise man who lays up knowledge is the one in communion with the one in whom all knowledge is hid, rather than producing from his own unreliable resources.
15: Theme. A rich man's wealth is his strong city; the poverty of the poor is their ruin — the economic-security observation. Christ's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) is the NT deconstruction of this observation: the strong city of wealth is shown to be no city at all on the night when the soul is required. The observation is sociologically accurate but spiritually incomplete — Proverbs itself offers the corrective in Proverbs 18:10-11 (the name of the LORD is the strong tower the righteous runs into). Christ is the strong tower that wealth pretends to be.
16: Theme. The wages of the righteous lead to life, the gain of the wicked to sin — the eschatological direction of work's fruit. Paul's wage-metaphor for justification in Romans 4:4-5 contrasts the earned wage with the gift of righteousness — but within the category of earned wages, Proverbs' observation stands: what the righteous produce leads toward life. Christ's work — his perfect obedience — earned the wage of life (resurrection) that he shares with those united to him, while the wage of sin is death (Romans 6:23), the trajectory the wicked's gain follows.
17: Theme. Whoever heeds instruction is on the path of life, but he who rejects reproof leads others astray — the instruction-path connection. Christ's instruction is the path of life in a direct sense: the words I have spoken to you are spirit and life (John 6:63). Heeding Christ's instruction is the NT form of what Proverbs identifies as the life-path. The one who rejects reproof leads others astray — the false teacher who misleads others is the maximum expression of this, and both 2 Pet 2 and Jude develop this warning in explicitly christological contexts.
18: Revelation of God. The one who conceals hatred has lying lips, and whoever utters slander is a fool — the two forms of verbal wickedness: concealed hatred and open slander. Christ's diagnostic is sharper: hatred and slander are already murder in the heart's economy (Matthew 5:21-22). He himself faced both — the concealed hatred of the Pharisees who sought to trap him while presenting as pious questioners, and the open slander that called him demon-possessed (John 8:48). His response to both was truth rather than retaliation.
19: Theme. When words are many, transgression is not lacking, but whoever restrains his lips is prudent — the economy of speech. Christ's instruction extends this to oaths: let your yes be yes and your no be no; anything more than this comes from evil (Matthew 5:37). The restraint of lips Proverbs commends is the speech-discipline of those formed by Christ's teaching — enough to be clear, no more than truth requires. James develops this in his sustained reflection on the tongue (Jas 3), which is essentially the NT commentary on this verse.
20: Type. The tongue of the righteous is choice silver; the heart of the wicked is of little worth — choice silver as the figure for valuable, refined speech. The metallurgical image: silver that has been smelted pure. Christ's words are described as the words of eternal life (John 6:68) — the tongue of the perfectly righteous one produces speech of incomparable value. The silver-tongue type points to the one whose every word is proven true (Proverbs 30:5), whose speech creates and sustains life (John 1:3; Proverbs 11:43).
21: Theme. The lips of the righteous feed many, but fools die for lack of sense — the feeding-by-speech principle. Christ's feeding ministry operates at multiple levels simultaneously: physical bread and fish for thousands, and the word that is true food (John 4:32; Proverbs 6:35). The righteous one whose lips feed many is fulfilled in the one who feeds the multitude and whose lips produce the bread-of-life discourse. Paul's ministry as feeding those he had led to faith (1 Corinthians 3:2; 1 Thessalonians 2:7-8) is the apostolic extension of this pattern.
22: Revelation of God. The blessing of the LORD makes rich, and he adds no sorrow with it — the sorrow-free blessing. The blessings human effort produces are mixed with the anxiety of maintenance and the grief of loss. Christ identifies the kingdom's gifts as the sorrow-free form: my joy I give you, not as the world gives (John 14:27; Proverbs 15:11). The peace and joy that are fruits of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22) are the NT form of the sorrow-free blessing Proverbs locates in the LORD's direct gift rather than in human accumulation.
23: Theme. Doing wrong is like a joke to a fool, but wisdom is pleasure to a man of understanding — the affective register of wisdom vs. folly. The fool finds transgression amusing — he has no interior alarm that registers moral wrongness as serious. Christ's teaching on the heart (Matthew 15:18-19) identifies this desensitization as the root problem: the hardened heart that produces evil is the heart that has lost the capacity for the moral seriousness Proverbs commends as pleasure in wisdom.
24: Theme. What the wicked dreads will come upon him, but the desire of the righteous will be granted — the granted-desire of the righteous. Christ's promise extends this: whatever you ask in my name, this I will do (John 14:13-14). The desire-granted is not a blank check but the prayer of those whose desires are formed by communion with Christ — their requests align with the Father's will because they are shaped by the Son's character. The wicked's dread-coming-upon-him is the judgment-reversal pattern of the beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12).
25: Type / Theme. When the tempest passes, the wicked is no more, but the righteous is established forever — the storm-survival formula. Christ's parable of the wise and foolish builders (Matthew 7:24-27) applies exactly this principle: when the storm (chevrona) comes, the house built on rock (tzur) stands while the house built on sand collapses. The wise builder is the one who hears and does Christ's words — the righteous established forever is the one whose foundation is Christ himself. The storm is both historical pressure and eschatological judgment.
26: Theme. Like vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is the sluggard to those who send him — the irritant-comparison. The disciples are sent (apostello) precisely to be reliable messengers (Matthew 10:16; John 17:18). The irresponsible messenger is the antithesis of the apostolic pattern Christ establishes — the sent-one who fails the sending represents a relational and missional failure, not merely an inconvenience. Paul's anxiety about being disqualified from the race (1 Corinthians 9:27) is the apostolic form of this concern.
27: Theme. The fear of the LORD prolongs life, but the years of the wicked will be cut short — longevity as the fruit of covenant faithfulness. Christ identifies himself as the resurrection and the life (John 11:25) — the one in whom the life-prolongation Proverbs promises to the God-fearer is ultimately secured, not merely extended. Eternal life is the maximum expression of what prolonged life points toward. The fear of the LORD that produces life is defined in John as knowing the Father and the Son (John 17:3).
28: Theme. The hope of the righteous brings joy, but the expectation of the wicked will perish — hope as a defining mark of the righteous. Paul's theology of hope (Romans 5:1-5; Proverbs 8:24-25) is grounded precisely in the resurrection of Christ: we have this hope as an anchor for the soul (Hebrews 6:19). The hope of the righteous brings joy because it is a settled orientation toward the certain future that God has secured in Christ's resurrection. The wicked's expectation perishes because it is grounded in present circumstances alone.
29: Revelation of God. The way of the LORD is a stronghold to the blameless, but destruction to evildoers — the LORD's way as protection for the blameless and destruction for the wicked. The same object (God's revealed will/path) produces opposite effects depending on the person who encounters it. This double-effect pattern is the hermeneutic Paul uses for the gospel: to one a fragrance from death to death, to the other a fragrance from life to life (2 Corinthians 2:16). Christ's cross is the most concentrated expression of the LORD's way — it is both salvation and judgment depending on the orientation of the one who encounters it (John 3:17-19).
30: Theme. The righteous will never be removed, but the wicked will not dwell in the land — the land-permanence of the righteous as covenant promise. Christ extends the land-promise to its eschatological scope: the meek shall inherit the earth (Matthew 5:5, citing Psalms 37:11). The removal of the wicked from the land is the eschatological judgment that separates the wheat from the chaff (Matthew 13:30). The righteous who are never removed are those whose citizenship is in the new Jerusalem — the land-promise transformed into an eternal inheritance (Hebrews 11:14-16).
31: Type. The mouth of the righteous brings forth wisdom, but the perverse tongue will be cut off — wisdom produced by the mouth of the righteous. The tree-fruit imagery (mouth bringing forth) and the cutting-off of the perverse tongue anticipates the vine-branch language of John 15: the branch that bears fruit remains; the one that does not is cut off and thrown into the fire (John 15:2, 6). Christ as the perfectly righteous one whose mouth produces wisdom is the Logos through whom all wisdom originates (Colossians 2:3) — and who cuts off what is perverse by the sword of his mouth (Revelation 19:15).
32: Type. The lips of the righteous know what is acceptable, but the mouth of the wicked, what is perverse — knowing what is acceptable to speak (ratzon, the LORD's good pleasure). Christ's speech is always characterized by its alignment with the Father's will: I have not spoken on my own authority, but the Father who sent me has himself given me a commandment — what to say and what to speak (John 12:49). The righteous man's lips that know what is acceptable are the type of the Son whose every word is the Father's word — the acceptable speech being the speech that is sourced in God rather than in the speaker's own inclinations.
